texts.label: text
texts.orig: Abstract /G2D Experimental studies of the solubility of carbon dioxide and hydrogen sulfide in aqueous solutions of alkanolamines, widely used industrial absorbents of acid gases, are reviewed. A comparative analysis of methods employed by various authors is made. The available published data on the solubility of CO 2 and H 2 S in solutions of primary, secondary, and tertiary alkanolamines are analyzed. Fundamental aspects of the influence exerted by various additives on the phase behavior of systems constituted by an acid gas, alkanolamine, and water are considered.
texts.text: Abstract /G2D Experimental studies of the solubility of carbon dioxide and hydrogen sulfide in aqueous solutions of alkanolamines, widely used industrial absorbents of acid gases, are reviewed. A comparative analysis of methods employed by various authors is made. The available published data on the solubility of CO 2 and H 2 S in solutions of primary, secondary, and tertiary alkanolamines are analyzed. Fundamental aspects of the influence exerted by various additives on the phase behavior of systems constituted by an acid gas, alkanolamine, and water are considered.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Data on phase equilibria in aqueous systems containing alkanolamines and acid gases, such as hydrogen sulfide and carbon dioxide, are primarily required for solving problems associated with purification of industrial gas flows to remove the above-mentioned noxious components. These problems are topical for numerous technological processes. In particular, hydrogen sulfide frequently occurs as accompanying components in oil-and-gas fields. Carbon dioxide is formed as a by-product in many chemical industries. Absorption of these gases is important both economically and ecologically.

texts.orig: After the first patent [1] for use of an aqueous solution of triethanolamine as an absorbent was received, a large number of studies concerned with the solubility of acid gases in aqueous solutions of alkanolamines have been reported. These nitrogencontaining compounds are widely used for absorption of acid gases. Technological schemes of industrial installations for gas purification with alkanolamine solutions and specific features of their application were described in detail in a book by Kohl and Nielsen [2].

One of variables commonly fixed in experiments with the systems under study is temperature, whose constancy is provided by thermostating. What variables are additionally fixed depends on a particular experimental technique. This may be the empirical composition of the system (total amounts of component moles in both phases), composition of the liquid phase, and partial pressure of volatile components in the gas phase. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Static method. Most of the available data on the solubility in the systems studied have been obtained by the static method [3 /G33 15]. The technique consists in that a thermostated system of a prescribed empirical composition is brought in thermodynamic equilibrium and the phase compositions and pressure are directly determined. The measurement procedure used in this method consists in the following. A known amount of an aqueous solution of an amine is placed in the cell, a required amount of carbon dioxide and (or) hydrogen sulfide is introduced, and the system is kept until equilibrium is established, after which values of the following thermodynamic parameters are recorded: equilibrium pressure and mole fractions of components in each of the coexisting phases.

texts.orig: In the study by Jones [3], a stainless steel cylinder placed in a thermostat served as a cell in which equilibrium was established. On one of its sides, the cylinder was equipped with a capillary with a valve for sampling the liquid phase. On its other side, it had a gage. A capillary for sampling the vapor phase was connected to the upper part of the cylinder. A similar cell design was used by Lawson and co-authors [5]. To agitate the contents of the cell, the installation was equipped with an attachment that rocked the cell during an experiment. In the later studies [7 /G33 9], the agitation was effected by a rabble placed within the cell and driven by a magnetic activator.

texts.orig: It should be noted that, in the case of H 2 S, equilibrium is attained rather rapidly because an amine can be protonated without any solvent (water), with hydrogen sulfide molecules serving as a source of protons. At the same time, the amine cannot be protonated in the case of CO 2 without involvement of water and equilibrium is attained considerably more slowly in systems with CO 2 . However, agitation is important in both cases, because it provides dissolution of fine gas bubbles, which could otherwise reside for a long time, e.g., at places where the capillaries are connected to the cell.

The installation employed in [10] comprised a glass cylinder with stainless steel flanges. Use of this cell variety enables a convenient visual inspection of the state of a system under study and allows measurements at both elevated and low partial pressures of CO 2 . Impeller rabbles served to agitate both the liquid and vapor phases, which favored a faster establishment of equilibrium.

texts.orig: In many cases, the composition of the vapor phase was determined by gas chromatography [4, 8] or mass spectrometry [3, 5]. Occasionally, another method has been used to analyze the vapor for the content of H2 S: a vapor sample was absorbed with a 25% solution of methyldiethanolamine and the content of sulfide ions was found by potentiometric titration with a NH 4 NO3 solution with a salt concentration of 0.1 M. The concentration of H 2 S in the liquid phase was determined in most cases by adding an excess amount of an acidified iodine solution, followed by back titration with a thiosulfate. In [11], small concentrations of H 2 S in the vapor phase were determined using Methylene Blue. In this method, sulfide ions react with paraaminodimethylamine to give Methylene Blue and then the coloration intensity is quantitatively measured with a spectrophotometer calibrated against standard sulfide solutions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Samples for determining the content of CO2 in the liquid phase were commonly taken into a NaOH solution, with the gas thereby converted into nonvolatile ionic forms. Further, these forms were precipitated as barium carbonate, which was then titrated with a sulfuric acid solution. In [4], samples for analysis for the content of CO 2 were taken into a vessel containing a weighed amount of sulfuric acid. The CO 2 evolved was collected into a graduated burette for determining the volume of the gas. This method is rather simple in use, but fails to provide good accuracy. In [3], the concentration of CO 2 in the liquid was found by the Knorr method modified by Scott. The method consists in that a sample is acidified with sulfuric acid and then heated. The CO2 evolved is washed out with nitrogen, dried, and passed through a tube packed with special Caroxite absorbent. The amount of the gas absorbed is found by weighing. If hydrogen sulfide is present, it is removed from the gas flow with an acidified solution of potassium permanganate. In [11], an AgNO 3 solution added in a minor excess was used to oxidize H2 S, instead of potassium permanganate. The content of the amine in solution was determined by titrating an aliquot of the liquid phase with sulfuric acid, with Methyl Red as indicator.

texts.orig: There exists an attractive variety of the static method, which does not require any analysis of equilibrium phases. This technique was used in [14, 15] in a study of methyldiethanolamine-containing aqueous solutions of CO 2 and H 2 S in the pressure range 100 /G33 8000 kPa. These measurements were performed at Prof. Maurer's laboratory at the chair of technical thermodynamics of the University of Kaiserslautern. The initial state of the system in the measuring cell is an aqueous solution of an amine of prescribed composition. In the course of an experiment, a certain amount of a gas is introduced under pressure into the cell and additional portions of an aqueous amine solution are introduced until the system comes to a homogeneous state. After equilibrium is attained, the pressure in the cell is gradually lowered by taking small samples of the liquid until first bubbles of the gas phase appear. The pressure is recorded at this instant of time. In this case, the amount of the gas phase is very small, and, therefore, the composition of the saturated liquid coincides with the general composition of the mixture. The advantages of the method consist in that phase analysis can be avoided, the expenditure of reagents is small, and the measurement accuracy is high.

texts.orig: Circulation method. Another technique widely used for studying the solubility of gases is the circulation method [16 /G33 22]. In this method, the saturation of an amine solution with a gas is provided by a circulation pump serving to bubble the gas through the liquid phase. The experimental installation includes a measuring cell equipped with a system for vapor circulation, vapor tank, cell for gas batching, and pressure transducers [17]. A constant temperature is maintained with a thermostat. As in the static method, the amounts of each of the components of the system are fixed; the equilibrium pressure and phase compositions are determined in the course of an experiment. The difference is in that the vapor is circulated and an inert component, nitrogen, is present in the system. At the beginning of an experiment, a weighed amount of a solution of water and of an amine of prescribed composition are placed in the measuring cell. Nitrogen is introduced into the cell (for further control over CO 2 batching) and, after the temperature is equilibrated, the equilibrium pressure, constituted by water and amine vapor pressures and nitrogen pressure, is recorded. Then a portion of CO2 is introduced into the cell by a special device. The amount of the gas introduced is determined from a change in the pressure in the batching vessel with the use of p /G33 V /G33 T data. The total pressure in the cell is measured and equilibrium is considered to be attained if the pressure remains constant during a long interval of time (say, 30 min). Commonly, this process takes 3 /G33 4 h. The equilibrium partial pressure of CO 2 is found as the difference of the total pressure and the equilibrium pressure recorded

texts.orig: The specific feature of the circulation technique used in [18] is in that a constant gas concentration in the vapor phase serves as an indication that equilibrium is attained. To determine this concentration, small vapor samples were taken so that changes in the composition of the liquid were negligible. A vapor sample was cooled to 283 K in order to condense water and the amine, and the content of CO 2 was determined with an IR analyzer. After the system came to equilibrium (in 2 /G33 3 h), both liquid and vapor phases were sampled for analysis.

texts.orig: Flow method. In addition to the static and circulation methods, the flow method [23 /G33 26] is occasionally used to study the solubility of gases. The specificity of this technique is in that the partial pressure of the acid gas in the gas flow is set in the course of an experiment and the composition of the liquid solution through which the gas flow is passed changes until the solution comes to equilibrium with the gas. Thus, the quantities set in the experiment are temperature, number of moles of water and an amine in the solution, and partial pressure of the gas (for definiteness, this will be CO 2 in what follows). A typical description of an installation of this kind can be found in [25].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The installation comprised two stainless steel cylinders placed in the vertical position in a water thermostat. The first cylinder, a water saturator, served to feed a gas saturated with a water vapor into the cell. The second cylinder contained the alkanolamine solution under study. A stainless steel pipe was inserted into the upper part of each of the cylinders. A capillary via which a gas flow was passed through the solution ran from this pipe into the cylinder. The flow contained, in addition to an acid gas under study, an inert carrier-gas (helium), which was necessary for providing a sufficient rate of solution saturation and for controlling the concentration of CO2 by varying the relative amounts of gaseous components delivered into the cell. An accurately calibrated flow regulator served to provide a stable flow of a gas mixture. In the course of an experiment, the content of CO2 in the input gas flow could be gradually changed using the flow regulator. The gas flow at the cell outlet was cooled to 273 K to remove water, after which the concentration of CO2 in the gas was determined with an IR analyzer. The analyzer was preliminarily calibrated against a CO 2 /G33 N2 mixture of a known composition. The equality of compositions of the gas mixture at the cell input and output served as an indication that equilibrium was attained between the gas phase and the solution. After these compositions became equal, the partial pressure of CO2 in the flow at the cell inlet was taken as the equilibrium partial pressure in the cell over the CO 2 /G33 amine /G33 water solution. The content of CO 2 in the saturated solution was found analytically. In summary of the description of experimental methods used to study the solubility of gases, distinctive features of each of these methods should be noted. The static method is sufficiently accurate, but requires, in some variants, a considerable expenditure of reagents. The technique enables studies under widely varying conditions. However, it cannot always be used in measurements at low gas concentrations, which is due to a significant contribution from the adsorption of gases of the apparatus surface to the experimental error.

The most widely used in the industry are aqueous solutions containing individual amines: monoethanolamine (MEA), diethanolamine (DEA), methyldiethanolamine (MDEA), and /G5B sterically hindered /G5D amines, as well as amine solutions with various modifying additives.

texts.orig: The efficiency of this amine as an absorbent of acid gases is illustrated by Fig. 1, in which the solubility of CO 2 in an aqueous solution of monoethanolamine is compared with the solubility of the same gas in pure water. It can be seen that presence of the amine in the aqueous solution markedly changes the run of the solubility isotherm of the gas. In the case of pure water, the isotherm is a curve along which the pressure strongly increases as the CO2 concentration grows, beginning at its zero concentration. In an aqueous solution of the amine, two portions can be distinguished in the solubility isotherms of acid gases. While the mole ratio of the gas and the amine remains markedly smaller than unity, the pressure increases only slightly with the gas concentration in the solution, because the amine is protonated and molecular forms of CO2 are converted into ionic forms. As the equilibrium concentrations of the ionic forms in the solution are reached, the curve abruptly changes its run, because further increase in the gas concentration is not accompanied by chemical transformations. In this region, dissolution occurs via physical absorption.

texts.orig: A most important specific feature of MDEA is its capacity for selective absorption of hydrogen sulfide from its mixture with carbon dioxide [48 /G33 50]. The selectivity of absorption is due in the given case to the kinetic factor, specifically, to the higher rate of the reaction of MDEA with H 2 S. This is associated with specific features of the mechanism of chemical interaction, already mentioned above. It should be recalled that these two compounds can react directly, without involvement of solvent molecules. As for CO2 , its reaction with MDEA requires an aqueous medium as a source of protons for formation of hydrocarbonate ions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: These data were obtained in a wide range of temperatures and pressures. This is due to the practical demand for information about the phase behavior of the systems under consideration in various conditions. Gases are for the most part absorbed in industrial installations in the temperature range 313 /G33 333 K. Absorption at lower temperatures is unfeasible from the standpoint of the process kinetics. In addition, the chemical reaction of the gases with the amines is exothermic. Accordingly, there inevitably occurs heating by the heat of the reaction. The temperature range 373 /G33 413 K is also of interest for the industry. At these temperatures, amine solutions are regenerated via desorption. At higher temperatures the amines decompose, which leads to corrosion of the equipment. However, in the case of thermally stable amines, such as MDEA, desorption is also performed at temperatures higher than 413 K.

texts.orig: Addition of primary and secondary amines improves the interaction kinetics, but makes higher the corrosion activity of the solvent. Here, too, the choice of the solvent is aimed to raise the absorption rate without introduction of additional negative factors. Piperazine is frequently used as a modifying additive. A high efficiency of CO2 absorption by the MDEA solution is achieved in the presence of piperazine. The effect of this compound is due, firstly, to its high capacity for protonation (one molecule of piperazine can add two protons). Secondly, piperazine can form three different carbamate ions with CO2 . Owing to this circumstance, its use in a mixture with MDEA for absorption of CO 2 is economically feasible. Addition of piperazine also has a positive effect in treatment of a mixture of carbon dioxide and hydrogen sulfide with an MDEA solution. In this case, piperazine improves the selectivity of hydrogen sulfide absorption.

texts.orig: One of the most widely used physical solvents is methanol. In [61], the effect of methanol on the solubility of CO2 in an aqueous solution of MEA was studied. Henni and Mather [62] examined the effect of methanol on the solubility of CO2 in an aqueous solution of MDEA and analyzed the solubility of the same gas in a nonaqueous solvent, MDEA /G33 methanol, containing 50% of each of the components. A pronounced improvement of the solubility of CO 2 in the presence of methanol was observed.

texts.orig: Another widely used physical solvent is tetrahydrothiophene-1,1-dioxide (sulfolane). In [63], the solubility of carbon dioxide and hydrogen sulfide in a solution with a patented name Sulfinol, which contained 40 wt % diisopropanolamine (DIPA), 40 wt % sulfolane, and 20 wt % water, was studied. McGregor et al. [52] analyzed the effect of sulfolane on the solubility of CO 2 and H 2 S in aqueous solutions of MDEA. In [64], the solubility of CO 2 in an aqueous solution of MDEA, simultaneously containing piperazine and sulfolane, was examined.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: One more example of solutions of this kind is ethylene glycol. A study by Song [65] was devoted to analysis of the influence exerted by this additive on the solubility of CO2 in an aqueous solution of monoethanolamine. It was found experimentally under what conditions addition of ethylene glycol improves the absorption capacity of the MEA solution to the greatest extent. The advantage of ethylene glycol also consists in that the selectivity of absorption of hydrogen sulfide from its mixture with carbon dioxide by an MDEA solution is improved. In this case, the selectivity is not due to the kinetic factor, as in to the case of the piperazine additive. Here, the positive property of ethylene glycol is manifested in that chemical binding of CO 2 by methyldiethanolamine is impossible without participation of water because of the lack of a sufficient number of protons. As for H2 S, it can react with MDEA even in a non-aqueous medium, because it is a source of protons by itself. A systematic study of the effect of ethylene glycol on the solubility of CO2 and H 2 S in aqueous solutions of MDEA was carried out in [66].

texts.orig: Obtaining quantitative information about the effect of electrolytes on the solubility of gases in MDEA solutions is an important task, because these compounds strongly affect the solubility of acid gases in aqueous solutions of the amine. As demonstrated by the results of [67, 68], this influence is of a rather complicated nature, although electrolytes are not directly involved in chemical reactions. In some cases and, in particular, at elevated temperatures, it is impossible to predict a priori even qualitatively the effect of electrolytes on the solubility of gases in amine solutions.

texts.orig: A strong difference between the effects of sodium sulfate and methyldiethanolammonium on the solubility of CO 2 and H 2 S in an aqueous solution of MDEA has been revealed [69]. The phenomena observed were interpreted with account of the ion sizes, different capacities for solvation, and ion association occurring in the solutions. Understanding of the effects observed furnishes an opportunity to prognosticate the phase behavior of the systems under study, which is important for optimization of industrial processes of absorption of CO2 and H2 S.

texts.orig: To assess the reliability of published data on the solubility of gases in aqueous solutions of MDEA, it is necessary to subject these data to a thorough analysis. The assessment of the data quality is aided by comparison of results obtained by different authors. However, either there are no published data for comparison or the comparison is difficult because the data to be compared are obtained in different studies under dissimilar conditions.

However, either there are no published data for comparison or the comparison is difficult because the data to be compared are obtained in different studies under dissimilar conditions.

texts.orig: In addition, reports on studies of mixed amine solutions frequently present data that are rather detailed, but inconvenient for a thermodynamic analysis. For example, the solubility of CO 2 and H 2 S in mixed solutions of AMP and DEA was studied in [22] at varied relative contents of the amines but constant total content of AMP and DEA, equal to 30 wt %. These data are undoubtedly useful for a particular industrial application, but it would be of real interest from the thermodynamic standpoint to have data obtained at a constant total number of moles of the amines in solution.

texts.orig: The verification of the accuracy of experimental data is well aided by thermodynamic models. However, the potentialities of these models are considerably higher. They enable correction of experimental data and prognostication of properties of systems under conditions for which there are no data. The important role of the models consists in that they relate the behavior of a system to molecular characteristics of its components and to contributions of physical and chemical interactions. The modeling of phase equilibria in aqueous systems containing alkanolamines and acid gases constitutes a separate research area in which a large number of studies have been carried out [70 73].

texts.orig: veloped. The choice of a particular technique is governed by specific conditions of a process being designed. There are sufficiently detailed published data on the solubility of gases in aqueous solutions of both individual amines and amines with additives improving the solubility. However, unreliable experimental results frequently occur, and attention should be given to the choice of a set of experimental data. As for the effect of additives that hinder the dissolution of acid gases, it has been studied in insufficient detail. For example, there are no data of the effect of additions of sodium sulfate and alkanolammonium in systems with primary, secondary, and sterically hindered amines. Information about the effect of other electrolytes cannot be found in the literature at all. Further studies are necessary in this area of research.
